metadata.title: Shabby Chic Furniture Distressing & Antiquing Techniques with Glaze and Stain
metadata.description:
http://Brini.TV ► Ask Brini Maxwell anything at ► http://AskBrini.com ◄ 

► http://facebook.com/Brini.TV ► http://twitter.com/BriniMaxwell ► More ▼

I'm proud to present the first finished portion of my renovation in this video. You'll see the process of painting and antiquing the vintage wall unit that comprises my desk. It's done in 7 easy steps - sanding, priming, painting, detailing, aging, speckling and sealing. I go into detail on aging, which is done with glaze tinted with paint in a dirty color that is spread on with a brush, then wiped in and off with a wet rag and speckling, which is done with stain and an almost dry, ratty old paint brush, which when hit against the shaft of a screwdriver, leaves beguiling little speckles on the surface of your furniture. Painting old Craigslist and Freecycle finds is great fun and an economical way to furnish your home with style. If you decide to try it, please share it with us in the form of a video response or at least some before and after pictures!

Why didn't you think of that?

Love,
Brini

Visit my official site for more info ► http://www.BriniMaxwell.com

Jane Napkins by Brini Maxwell ► http://amzn.to/JaneNapkins

Glazed Napkins by Brini Maxwell ► http://amzn.to/GlazedNapkins

ABOUT BRINI MAXWELL:

Described as part Donna Reed, part Mary Tyler Moore, Maxwell makes kitsch feel classy through her unparalleled personal flair for home design, entertaining and savvy household tips. Inspired by a divine thrift shop purchase of 1950's nesting bowls, she first began sharing her vintage/classic know-how with other Manhattanites in 1998 through her self-titled cable access television show. With an emphasis on uncompromising fabulousness, Brini quickly garnered a devoted fan base and established herself as the go-to-girl on vintage fashion and mid-century modern treasures. After five years on the local airwaves her show was picked up by the Style Network. The subsequent series has been called a delightful success and has attracted a diverse audience thorough its national platform.

Brini on the Web ► http://www.BriniMaxwell.com/

Brini on YouTube ►  http://Brini.TV

Brini on Facebook ►  http://facebook.com/Brini.TV

Brini on Twitter ► http://twitter.com/BriniMaxwell

Jane Napkins by Brini ► http://amzn.to/JaneNapkins

The Brini Maxwell Show

The pilot for the original show was produced in 1996. The show debuted on Manhattan Neighborhood Network on January 1, 1998, and aired for 5 years featuring tips, recipes, entertaining ideas, craft projects, home renovation and interior design schemes. It was produced by Sander's production company V.R.U.S.P. Inc. and directed by Sander's mother, Mary Jane Wells.

In 2001 Sander was contacted by Amy Briamonte, east coast director of development for west coast based Termite Art Productions (now Creative Differences Productions). Briamonte and Sander developed a pitch for the show for the Bravo network and received an order for a pilot. The pilot was produced in the summer of 2002 for a show titled Charming, Needs Work. The premise for the show was Maxwell lived in an apartment that she redecorated every week. After Bravo passed on the show Briamonte, Sander and Termite Art pitched it to Stephen Schwartz and Heather Moran, newly named VP's of programming for the Style network. The pitch resulted in an order for a 13-episode season.

The first season of The Brini Maxwell Show for the Style network was taped in the summer of 2003 with studio production taking place on the main stage at Unitel Studios on west 57th St. in New York City. Location shoots for the season were taped over a period of 6 months in New York City, Los Angeles and Las Vegas.

nodes.plm_action: Painting and waxing wooden frame using brush and cloth
nodes.plm_caption:
The video features a woman restoring a vintage 1950s credenza.
It shows different stages of the painting process and ends with a photo of the finished product.
The woman is very charming and informative.

summary.brief: Brini Maxwell restores a vintage wall‑unit credenza, antiquing it with glaze, stain and speckles, seals it, and then shows the finished piece in her home office before the video ends with credits.
summary.detailed: The video opens with Brini introducing an update on her renovation; the old finish on a Craigslist wall‑unit has already been stripped, sanded with coarse then fine grit and primed with an oil‑based primer. She paints a light base coat and adds a little yellow‑green detail, then mixes a paint‑tinted glaze (called enduring bronze) and, after dipping a brush, wipes it on with a slightly damp rag and wipes it off to give a subtle aged look. She rough‑sands the edges with 60‑grit sandpaper, re‑applies the bronze‑tinted glaze and wipes it in for a historic patina. Next she brushes a walnut‑colored stain onto a shelf, brushes most of it off, and creates tiny speckles by lightly tapping a paintbrush against a screwdriver. After the speckles dry, she seals the entire unit with polycrylic to prevent yellowing. The completed credenza is styled with books, drawer‑handle antiquing, a Formica top and vintage wallpaper, and Brini displays it in a tidy home‑office setting, pointing out drawers and surrounding décor. The segment ends with a quick room showcase, a closing shot of Brini, and end‑credit slides.

action.detailed: Sand the wood, prime it, paint the base coat and yellow‑green details, apply a paint‑tinted glaze and wipe it in with a damp rag, rough‑sand the edges, apply a bronze‑tinted glaze and wipe it in, stain the shelf and brush off excess, tap a brush against a screwdriver to create speckles, then seal the piece with polycrylic.

nodes.plm_action: Removing loose paint with fine sandpaper
nodes.plm_caption:
A woman in a red and white striped shirt is painting the side of a light-colored wooden piece ( maybe a cabinet door). She is using white pain and a paintbrush. She is standing in a room in front of a beige wall. There is a jar of paint nearby.
The woman is also wearing a black belt tied in a bow, dark denim pants and a white scarf tied around her head.
The woman stands behind a table and she is using her right hand to hold the wooden piece while she paints the side of it with her left hand holding the brush and the white container of paint. She takes the brush and moves it up and down all over the surface of the wood and she puts the white cloth over the brush and moves it all over the sides of the wooden piece.
The room is a bedroom and the walls are white.

summary.brief: Brini Maxwell sands, primes, paints, ages and speckles a vintage wall unit before sealing it.
summary.detailed: The video opens with Brini noting that the wall unit’s old finish has been stripped and all dust cleaned up. She sands the wood, first with an orbital sander and then by hand using coarse‑grit sandpaper on the edges and fine‑grit on the faces, wiping away the debris. After the surface is smooth she applies an oil‑base primer and lets it dry, then paints the entire piece and adds yellow‑green glaze mixed with paint, which she brushes on, lets set briefly and wipes off with a slightly damp rag to give a subtle, painterly patina. She begins the antiquing stage by rough‑up the edges with 60‑ grit sandpaper, applies a bronze‑tinted glaze, and again wipes it with a damp rag so the exposed wood takes on a soft aged look. Next she dips a ragged brush into a dark stain, brushes off the excess and lightly taps the brush tip against a screwdriver, scattering tiny speckles that mimic dust settled in the grain. Finally she mentions that the piece should be sealed with polycrylic (or polyurethane) to protect the finish, though the sealing is not shown in the footage.

action.detailed: Apply a bronze‑tinted glaze and wipe it with a damp rag to create a subtle aged surface, then create speckles by tapping a stain‑laden brush against a screwdriver.

nodes.plm_caption:
The video shows a living room or an office/ study where a woman is sitting on a red chair in front of a desk that contains a printer and a desktop.
At the beginning of the video, a woman stands next to a wooden structure, with both her hands flat on top. She then is shown sitting down next to a drawer, where she touches it with both hands, then the same drawer appears on different frames, before the woman reappears sitting on the chair. It also shows the drawer with a drawer in it, as well as different angles of the room.
At the end of the video, the woman reappears sitting in front of a similar drawer, talking to the camera, with a variety of graphics at the end of the video that shows information and an artist's signature.

summary.brief: The presenter displays the finished antiqued wall unit with its shelves, handles, Formica top, wallpaper and lighting, then ends with closing credits.
summary.detailed: The segment opens with Brini Maxwell standing beside the newly finished wall unit. She runs her hands over the surface, points out the speckled drawer handles that were antiqued, and shows the Formica top that extends the work surface. She places a few books on the shelves, highlights the vintage wallpaper that was applied to the wall behind the unit, and demonstrates the lighting fixture that was also antiqued. While speaking, she invites viewers to try the antiquing technique, asks them to send photos or video responses, and signs off with a friendly farewell. The video then transitions to a series of bright‑colored end‑card graphics that include a portrait of Brini in a floral dress and production credits.

action.detailed: Pulls back to display the completed antiqued wall unit, points out the speckled drawer handles, places books on the shelves, adjusts the Formica top, and gestures toward the wallpaper and lighting fixture while speaking.
action.actor: Brini Maxwell, the woman presenting the finished furniture

nodes.plm_action: Applying light brown paint with a flat paintbrush
nodes.plm_caption:
In this video a woman can be seen working with wood. She sand s a wooden surface first using a large sandpaper sander then sanding a piece of wood with a handheld sander and later sanding a piece of wood with a grit sandpaper. She then applies wood stain to a piece of wood, which has been primed. Finally, she demonstrates painting a piece of wood.
The background contains several pieces of wood, and a white paintbrush and sandpaper. The woman is standing on the left of the frame.

summary.brief: Brini Maxwell sands, primes, paints and adds a yellow‑green glaze to a reclaimed wall unit.
summary.detailed: After a brief title sequence of colorful flower graphics and the Brini Maxwell logo, the host in a yellow floral shirt stands beside a stripped wall unit that she has sanded and cleaned. She applies an oil‑based primer, notes its smell, then paints the entire unit with a base coat. Next she brushes on a decorative yellow‑green glaze that is mixed with paint, creating a slightly uneven, painterly finish on the details of the piece.

action.detailed: Prime the stripped unit with oil‑base primer, coat it with a base layer of paint, and then brush on a yellow‑green glaze mixed with paint to add decorative detailing.
action.actor: Brini Maxwell, the host wearing a yellow floral shirt

nodes.plm_caption:
This video shows a woman creating a wooden piece. She applies wax and paints the wooden frame. She also sands the wooden pieces.
This woman is painting a wooden frame. She applies white wax first and then a dark brown color. To sand the piece, she uses a small hand sandpaper and then a tool with small brushes.  The hand brush helps to add fine details to the surface while the larger brush handles more extensive tasks.
The small brush in the middle of the video appears to be a flat brush, while the larger brush that she used. The larger brush that she used after the wax is a sash brush and then a filbert brush.

summary.brief: The presenter ages a freshly painted wall unit by sanding, glazing, staining, speckling and sealing it.
summary.detailed: After completing the base paint and adding a yellow‑green glaze detail, the woman rough‑sands the painted edges with coarse 60‑grit sandpaper and brushes off the dust. She then dips a brush into a bronze‑tinted glaze, wipes the brush almost dry, and drags it across the wood, using a slightly damp rag followed by a dry rag to create a subtle, uneven aged tone. Next she applies a dark walnut stain, wipes away excess with a rag, and creates realistic speckles by loading a ratty brush with the stain, tapping the brush tip against a screwdriver and lightly brushing the droplets onto the surface. Finally she seals the entire piece with a clear polycrylic finish. Throughout the segment the work area is protected by white plastic sheeting, various cloths and brushes are used, and colorful flower graphics appear on the screen.

action.detailed: Rough‑sand the painted edges with 60‑grit sandpaper, brush off the dust, dip a brush in bronze‑tinted glaze and wipe it onto the wood, smooth it with a damp then dry rag, apply dark walnut stain and wipe the excess, create tiny speckles by tapping a stain‑laden brush against a screwdriver and brushing the droplets onto the surface, and finally seal the piece with polycrylic.
action.actor: The woman (Brini Maxwell) performing the antiquing work
